digests.narrative:
The crash was the test and the thesis passed it: photonics sold off 30–50% on deleveraging (his own leveraged book drew down 49%), then staged a historic single-day recovery — while every fundamental input improved. Goldman raised Innolight's 2026–2028 earnings estimates a stunning 65%/108%/119% on higher silicon-photonics volumes and 1.6T/3.2T ASPs, and Innolight itself says 800G demand is growing beyond expectations; he treats Innolight as the TSM-style read-through for the whole optical sector, so this is his demand thesis being marked to market by the sell side.

The CW-laser bottleneck now has independent confirmation from both ends: Lumentum is buying CW lasers off the open market per its own earnings transcript, and AMD is signing LTAs to secure CW capacity per TrendForce. Against that he published his most falsifiable Sivers work yet — a capacity model where 10% of Win Semi allocation at 65% yield and $50–75 ASP supports $341–512M of annual array revenue (~$205–307M gross profit against a ~$1.1B market cap). His template is Lumentum's own history: $3B to $60B+ in two years on the EML chokepoint; he argues SIVE sits before the same move on the CW chokepoint. The AAOI case got its arithmetic too: the company's own $471M/month estimate from H2 2027 against an $8B market cap is what he calls 'extremely stupid' valuation. Next dated catalyst: AMD's SF event, where an MI500 CPO commitment via Ayar or GF SCALE would map a second accelerator vendor onto his supply chain.
keyPoints: GS raises Innolight 2026–28 estimates 65%/108%/119% on SiPh volumes — the sector bellwether validates the demand thesis
800G demand 'growing more significantly than expected' per Innolight's own transcript
CW bottleneck double-confirmed: Lumentum buying CW off the open market, AMD signing CW LTAs
SIVE capacity model: 10% Win Semi allocation ≈ $341–512M revenue vs ~$1.1B MC — checkable arithmetic, not narrative
AAOI's own $471M/month H2 2027 estimate vs $8B MC is his 'extremely stupid' valuation case
Crash-and-recovery read: liquidation cascades set prices while fundamentals improved

digests.narrative:
The shortage got sell-side math this week: Meritz Securities argues suppliers can fulfill only 75–80% of DRAM demand in H2 2026, deteriorating to the 60% range in 2027 — 'this is not the time to sell Samsung and SK Hynix' — with market forecasts putting SK Hynix at 3.5x and Samsung at 3.9x 2027 P/E. Intel's CEO frames the duration: no relief in memory supply or pricing until at least 2028. Micron added a QCOM LTA to its 16+ agreements and fell 5% on the day, which he reads as the tail end of deleveraging rather than anything wrong with memory — a read the historic recovery day (Kioxia +17%, Samsung +6%, MU +12.5%, SNDK +12.8%) promptly supported.

His structure preferences are unchanged: the sharpest sub-cycles (SLC NAND forecast +120–170% H2 2026) accrue to smaller players because Micron's scale dwarfs those TAMs, and LTA price ceilings still cap how much of the spot spike the flagship captures. The SanDisk angle gained a qualification datapoint: AEHR completed a NAND burn-in benchmark with a 'global leader in NAND flash' now evaluating an HBM/NAND development agreement — he reads it as SanDisk and ties it to the HBF discussions. Housekeeping note he flags: SK Hynix ADRs become convertible with local shares July 29, which should compress the 25%+ ADR premium.
keyPoints: Meritz: DRAM fulfillment only 75–80% of demand H2 2026, falling to ~60% range in 2027
Intel CEO: no relief in memory supply or pricing until at least 2028
MU adds a QCOM LTA to 16+ agreements — and falls 5%; he calls it deleveraging, not demand
AEHR NAND burn-in benchmark (his read: SanDisk) opens an HBM/NAND qualification path
SLC NAND +120–170% H2 2026 accrues to smaller players; LTA ceilings still cap flagship capture
SKHY ADR/local convertibility July 29 should compress the 25%+ ADR premium

digests.narrative:
The fab layer's week ran crash-to-catalyst. The rout took the whole layer down with the deleveraging cascade, then the recovery day repriced it violently (TSEM +10.4%, INTC +8.2%) — supporting his consistent read that the selling was liquidity, not order books. Intel's role in his corpus stays instrumental: its CEO's 'no relief in memory supply or pricing until at least 2028' is his favorite duration quote for the memory cycle, and 'INTC to LITE' is the span of his market-bottom gauge.

The forward event is AMD's SF disclosure: Jefferies expects scale-up CPO roadmap details for MI500, and GlobalFoundries is reportedly involved via GF SCALE — while his Sivers capacity modeling lists GFS among the fabs the laser ramp runs through. A public AMD commitment to GF SCALE would give the silicon-photonics foundry story its first named anchor customer, upgrading the platform frame (TSM/GFS/TSEM as the SiPho capacity layer) from sell-side map to design win. The durable value idea is untouched: XFAB as the cheapest specialty foundry with the photonixfab option attached.
keyPoints: AMD SF event: Jefferies expects MI500 CPO roadmap details — GF SCALE reportedly involved
A named AMD/GF SCALE win would be the SiPho foundry story's first public anchor customer
Recovery day repriced the layer (TSEM +10.4%, INTC +8.2%) — liquidity selling, not order books
INTC used as instrument: memory-duration quote and the 'INTC to LITE' bottom gauge
XFAB cheapest-specialty-foundry value case with the photonixfab free option — unchanged

digests.narrative:
The strongest neocloud week in the corpus. IREN revised its ARR target to $4B+ on new AI cloud contracts with a customer base now spanning Microsoft, Nvidia, Perplexity, and Figure (+19.7%); HUT signed a 15-year $9.8B AI datacenter lease (+10.5%); and the whole cohort recovered sharply off the crash. Above them, two structural validations: NVDA disclosed 9.3% beneficial ownership of Nebius via SEC filing — not new money (existing shares plus a prefunded warrant) but now official, putting the industry's most important balance sheet visibly behind the 'Neocloud leader' — and META entered talks to lease $10B of compute to Anthropic, which he reads as hyperscalers copying the neocloud homework after seeing how profitable the SpaceX compute deal was.

His discipline survives the euphoria: he likes IREN fundamentally post-Mirantis (software orchestration plus secured gigawatt capacity) but won't touch it until executive stock-based compensation and financing mechanics are addressed, and the Reflection-style counterparty-funding caution on LTA books still stands. The desk adds the caveat he skips: a hyperscaler renting out compute validates the model today and competes with it tomorrow.
keyPoints: IREN revises ARR target to $4B+; customers now include Microsoft, Nvidia, Perplexity, Figure
HUT signs a 15-year, $9.8B AI datacenter lease
NVDA discloses 9.3% beneficial ownership of Nebius — sentiment anchor, not new money
META–Anthropic $10B compute-lease talks: hyperscalers copying the neocloud model
His IREN discipline: likes fundamentals post-Mirantis, blocked on SBC and financing mechanics
Counterparty fundability (the Reflection pattern) remains the quality filter on contract books

digests.narrative:
AEHR followed its +36% earnings pop with a +26.5% recovery-day move — the theme's qualification-breadth story (AI processors, silicon photonics, NAND/HBM burn-in) is now attached to one of the most violent price series in the book, and he flags exactly that: $116 to $60 to $94 in a month means you'd better know what you hold. The NAND benchmark customer ('global leader in NAND flash', now evaluating an HBM/NAND development agreement) is his bridge between this theme and the memory book — he reads it as SanDisk.

Upstream, he published a component-bottleneck timeline map sourced from Digitimes: ABF substrates about a year out, HDI boards 6+ months, CCL in severe material shortage (HVLP copper foil, glass fiber/cloth), and passives (MLCCs, chip resistors, tantalum and aluminum electrolytic capacitors) running 15–20 weeks to a year-plus — with the caveat that commodity and AI-datacenter grades mix in those numbers. It's chokepoint cartography rather than a trade: the named beneficiaries are mostly Asian-listed (Ibiden, Elite Material, Murata), with only scattered US-ticker exposure.
keyPoints: AEHR +26.5% on the recovery day after the +36% earnings move — qualification breadth meets violent beta
AEHR NAND burn-in benchmark → HBM/NAND development agreement evaluation (his read: SanDisk)
Bottleneck timelines: ABF ~1 year, HDI 6+ months, CCL severe (copper foil, glass cloth)
Passives (MLCC, resistors, tantalum/aluminum caps) at 15–20 weeks to 1 year+ lead times
Most named beneficiaries are Asian-listed — a map of chokepoints more than a US trade list

digests.narrative:
The theme's week was his component-bottleneck timeline post: multilayer boards ~6 months (WUS, TTMI, Victory Giant, Gold Circuit), HDI boards 6+ months, CCL over 6 months with severe raw-material shortages, and chip resistors/capacitors stretching toward a year — with VSH appearing twice (resistors and tantalum caps). It extends the PCB-shortage-to-2028 frame with named suppliers and dated lead times, but stays cartography: no valuation work, no position.

Marvell remains the unresolved name: down 31% on the month, +7.3% on the recovery day, and every mention this week was a market-breadth list post. The MS optical-engine placement is still the only substantive datapoint; the dedicated, numbers-driven networking thesis still hasn't been written.
keyPoints: Bottleneck timelines with named suppliers: multilayer/HDI boards 6+ months, CCL severe, passives up to 1 year+
VSH named twice (chip resistors, tantalum caps); WUS and TTMI on multilayer boards
PCB shortage-to-2028 frame extended with dated lead times — still cartography, not a trade
MRVL: -31% month, +7.3% recovery day, still no dedicated thesis — list-post presence only

digests.narrative: One clarifying datapoint: CPO hasn't scaled yet, and much of the Corning story rides the 2028 FAU TAM — so the recent CPO delay-report cycle and glass-bridge uncertainty hit exactly where GLW's optics earnings live. His framing stays consistent with the MS recap: the glass bridge has potential in the CPO stack but displacing incumbent FAU solutions near-term is hard. Tracked, not owned; the theme stays a placeholder until CPO volumes actually arrive.
keyPoints: GLW's optics case leans on the 2028 FAU TAM — CPO hasn't scaled yet
CPO delay-report cycle and glass-bridge uncertainty hit the earnings timeline directly
Still no dedicated glass-substrate thesis — tracked, not owned

digests.narrative:
The theme got its first institutional scaffolding: an IBK Research report on Boston Dynamics value chains that he mostly uses as a measuring stick. He rejects their linear volume ramp (+10K units/year) for an S-curve — his own guess is 90–140K units by 2030 versus IBK's 30K — anchored on Boston Dynamics' 30K capacity target for 2028 and China already collectively shipping ~100K by end-2026. The report's most useful claim, in his view: actuator capacity is the cleanest forward indicator of the ramp (~310K actuators per 10K robots), making actuator output, yields and ASPs the thing to track through 2028–2030.

TSLA supplied the first hard production datapoint of the cycle: procurement guidelines requiring suppliers to hit 1,000 Optimus gen-3 units/week by September, doubling to 2,000–2,500 by year end. His conviction expression is still Agility via CCXI ('own as much % as possible' — maximum book-talking), with the IBK landscape placing Agility among the US leaders against Unitree/Figure/Apptronik and the Chinese volume machine.
keyPoints: IBK/Boston Dynamics report gives the theme institutional valuation scaffolding
He rejects linear ramps: his 2030 guess is 90–140K units vs IBK's 30K
Actuator capacity (~310K per 10K robots) is the leading indicator to track
TSLA Optimus gen-3 supplier mandates: 1,000/week by Sept, 2,000–2,500 by year end
Agility (via CCXI) remains his conviction pick — with heavy book-talking discount

digests.narrative:
The Vera Rubin rumor cycle closed the way he bet it would: Jensen's flat Tokyo denial held, and the supply-chain stocks the rumor damaged snapped back in a historic single-day recovery — the clearest demonstration yet of his point that narrative risk at NVDA is the real risk, borne mostly by its suppliers. NVDA also became a disclosed 9.3% beneficial owner of Nebius, extending the ecosystem-shareholder pattern.

AMD had the bigger week: a $5B investment in Anthropic paired with a 2GW chip deal — openly the NVDA/Anthropic playbook copied — and the SF event where Jefferies expects MI500 scale-up CPO roadmap disclosures. His read: if AMD commits to CPO, the optical transition stops being an NVDA-only architecture bet, which is thematically bullish for the entire photonics chain; and if AMD names Ayar Labs or GF SCALE for the optical engine, his Sivers supply-chain map gains a second anchor. He is explicit that it's too early to call before the disclosures land.
keyPoints: Vera Rubin delay cycle closed: Jensen's denial held, suppliers snapped back in a historic recovery
AMD invests $5B in Anthropic with a 2GW chip deal — the NVDA playbook, copied
AMD SF event: MI500 CPO commitment would make the optical transition a two-vendor architecture
An Ayar/GF SCALE optical-engine naming would map SIVE upstream of a second accelerator vendor
NVDA discloses 9.3% of Nebius — the ecosystem-shareholder pattern extends

digests.narrative:
The capex story crossed into monetization: META is in talks to lease compute to Anthropic in a $10B deal, which he reads as Meta copying the economics it watched the SpaceX compute arrangement prove out. That reframes hyperscaler buildout from cost center to rentable asset — validation for the neocloud model and, the desk would add, eventual competition for it. Alongside the confirmed SNDK LTA and the Micron agreement book, Meta remains the name whose spending underwrites his memory and photonics positions.

The Apple thread is still open: no leaks yet on which AI chip companies AAPL is buying, but his working theory holds — Apple learned self-reliance from watching Google cut Meta off — and an Apple capex wave remains the unpriced upside scenario for the whole demand layer. Google's role in the corpus stays what it was: the demand gauge whose compute constraint proves the hunger is structural, not a stock idea; the desk rotated it off the verdict roster this week on exactly that basis.
keyPoints: META–Anthropic $10B compute-lease talks: capex becomes a revenue line, not just a cost
His read: Meta copying the proven SpaceX compute-deal economics
Meta's SNDK/Micron LTA book keeps underwriting the memory upcycle
AAPL chip-M&A thread open — an Apple capex wave is the unpriced fifth-payer scenario
GOOGL stays the corpus's demand gauge; rotated off the desk roster on tier math
